overview.market_size_source: Global Market Insights via Capstone Partners
overview.growth_rate: Steady growth driven by replacement cycles, energy efficiency regulations, and electrification trends
overview.fragmentation: Highly fragmented — dominated by independent, family-owned operators. Residential segment midway through consolidation; commercial segment still early-stage
overview.recurring_revenue_note: Companies with 40%+ revenue from service agreements command 0.5x–1.0x higher multiples
overview.pe_interest: PE deal share rose from 8% in 2023 to 23% in 2024. Add-on transactions up 88% YoY through June 2025.
overview.deal_volume_2024: 138 announced or completed deals (CapIQ, 2024); 60 in H1 2025
overview.labor_challenge: ~110,000 unfilled technician positions; ~23,000 technicians exit annually
overview.ai_disruption_risk: Low — skilled trades largely untouchable by AI/automation

$2M-$5M.source: First Page Sage 2025 / Axial Platform Data
$2M-$5M.notes: Small HVAC transactions typically fall in the 3x–5x range; well-run operators with service contracts can reach 6x+

$5M-$10M.source: First Page Sage 2025 / PKF O'Connor Davies
$5M-$10M.notes: Sweet spot for PE add-on acquisitions. Midsize HVAC companies averaging ~8.5x EBITDA haven't seen much fluctuation with interest rate changes.

$10M-$15M.notes: Platform-quality businesses at this size. Transaction multiples for high-revenue visibility businesses remain north of 10x.

$15M-$25M.notes: Companies with $50M+ revenue averaged ~9x EBITDA (2019-2023). At $15M-$25M EBITDA, platform-scale businesses with strong service components command 10x+.

value_drivers.explanation: Companies with 40%+ revenue from service agreements command significantly higher multiples. Recurring revenue provides cash flow predictability PE buyers prize.
High customer concentration increases risk for buyers, especially in commercial HVAC where losing a major contract can significantly impact revenue.
~52% of HVAC companies that go to market do not sell, often due to high owner dependence. PE firms specifically seek management teams capable of operating independently.
Dense service territories improve technician utilization and reduce windshield time. PE buyers value geographic clustering for add-on integration.
W-2 technician model preferred by sophisticated buyers — provides operational control and reduces misclassification risk. Subcontractor-heavy models face regulatory scrutiny.
Companies showing 10-15% annual growth typically receive higher multiples. Growth signals market demand and management capability.
Commercial HVAC is still early-stage in consolidation, offering more runway. Commercial contracts tend to be larger and longer-duration.
Deferred capex on fleet and equipment reduces perceived value. Buyers discount for near-term replacement needs.
CRM, dispatching software, fleet management, and smart thermostat integration signal operational maturity. Reduces integration cost for acquirers.

recent_transactions.rationale: 8th acquisition for the Exigent platform since 2022. Commercial and industrial HVAC/mechanical services.
Chicago-based Prospect Partners exited its investment in this provider of custom, highly engineered HVAC systems.
Platform acquisition to penetrate existing end markets and broaden product portfolio in HVAC filtration.
Commercial facility services platform formed Nov 2020. Focus on family-owned HVAC/R, plumbing, and DTO businesses.

recent_transactions.acquirer: Sila Heating & Air Conditioning (Morgan Stanley Capital Partners)
recent_transactions.rationale: Residential HVAC roll-up backed by Morgan Stanley Capital Partners since May 2021. 28 completed acquisitions.
M&A strategy focused on high-growth Southeast region expansion.
